---
pagetitle: "EpiAware — composable tools for infectious disease modelling"
description: "A composable Julia ecosystem for infectious disease modelling."
toc: false
page-layout: custom
section-divs: false
hide-description: true
listing:
  - id: news-listing
    contents: news/posts
    max-items: 3
    sort: "date desc"
    fields: [date, title, description]
---

:::::: {.side-space}

<!-- Header -->
::: {.logo-header}

{{< include _includes/header.qmd >}}

:::
<!-- Header End Here -->

<!-- What is EpiAware Section -->
::: {.what-is-turing-grid .section-start-space .section-end-space}

::: {.feature-text-column}

::: {.feature-item}
::: {.feature-item-header}

```{=html}
<span class="feature-icon icon-composable"></span>
```

##### Composable

:::

Small, interchangeable parts — delays, latent processes, infection and observation models — combine into one joint model. Swap a part to test an assumption, keeping a single rigorous model instead of chaining separate ones.

:::

::: {.feature-item}
::: {.feature-item-header}

```{=html}
<span class="feature-icon icon-uncertainty"></span>
```

##### Uncertainty-aware

:::

Uncertainty propagated correctly from the latent process through infection dynamics and observation.

:::

::: {.feature-item}
::: {.feature-item-header}

```{=html}
<span class="feature-icon icon-response"></span>
```

##### Built for response

:::

Designed for real-time outbreak analysis: nowcasting, forecasting, and scenario modelling from the same model definition, with reporting delays and ascertainment built in.

:::

::: {.feature-item}
::: {.feature-item-header}

```{=html}
<span class="feature-icon icon-julia"></span>
```

##### Built on Julia

:::

Fast, autodiff-ready, and tightly integrated with Turing.jl, SciML, and the Julia scientific ecosystem.

:::

::: {.feature-item}
::: {.feature-item-header}

```{=html}
<span class="feature-icon icon-approaches"></span>
```

##### Multiple approaches

:::

Aims to support multiple approaches to composing infectious disease models — [composing distributions](tutorials/composing-distributions.qmd) into event trees, or [assembling a full model from parts](tutorials/assembled-model.qmd).

:::

:::

::: {.code-example-column}
::: {.code-window .box}
::: {.code-window-header}
::: {.code-window-dots}

```{=html}
<span class="dot red"></span><span class="dot yellow"></span><span class="dot green"></span>
```

::: 

::: {.code-window-title}

```{=html}
<span>composed_delays.jl</span>
```

:::

:::

```{.julia .code-overflow-scroll}
using ComposedDistributions, Distributions

# BDBV-inspired delay tree: parallel pathways
# with Sequential, Resolve, and Compete nested together
tree = compose((
    clinical = sequential(
        :onset_admit => Gamma(1.2, 3.0),
        :admit_resolve => resolve(
            :death => (Gamma(2.0, 3.5), 0.3),
            :discharge => (Gamma(1.0, 8.0), 0.7))),
    surveillance = sequential(
        :onset_notif => Gamma(0.7, 20.0),
        :notif_compete => compete(
            :confirmed => Gamma(2.0, 1.0),
            :lost => Gamma(5.0, 0.5))),
))

# Inspect tree, params, and default priors
event_tree(tree)
params_table(tree)
priors = build_priors(params_table(tree))

# Draw a synthetic case, or evaluate a real one's likelihood
rand(tree)
logpdf(tree, ans)
```

:::
:::

:::
<!-- What is EpiAware Section Ends Here -->


<!-- Core Packages Section -->
:::: {.section-header .section-start-space}

::: {#core-packages-header-content}

### Core Packages
<p>The EpiAware ecosystem is built on a foundation of powerful, composable packages.</p>

:::

```{=html}
<a href="packages/index.qmd" class="button desktop-only-button">Explore Ecosystem &rarr;</a>
```

::::

::::: {.core-packages-grid .section-end-space}

::: {.package-card .box #core-card}

##### [ComposableTuringIDModels.jl <i class="bi bi-box-arrow-up-right" style="font-size: 0.4em;"></i>](packages/composableturingidmodels.qmd){.panel-title}
<p>Assemble infectious disease models from interchangeable parts as a single Turing model.</p>

:::

::: {.package-card .box #core-card}

##### [CensoredDistributions.jl <i class="bi bi-box-arrow-up-right" style="font-size: 0.4em;"></i>](packages/censoreddistributions.qmd){.panel-title}
<p>Handle the censoring and truncation biases common in epidemiological delay data.</p>

:::

::: {.package-card .box #core-card}

##### [ComposedDistributions.jl <i class="bi bi-box-arrow-up-right" style="font-size: 0.4em;"></i>](packages/composeddistributions.qmd){.panel-title}
<p>A grammar for composing distributions into event trees.</p>

:::

::: {.package-card .box #core-card}

##### [ModifiedDistributions.jl <i class="bi bi-box-arrow-up-right" style="font-size: 0.4em;"></i>](packages/modifieddistributions.qmd){.panel-title}
<p>Rescale, weight, or modify distributions, keeping the full distribution interface.</p>

:::

:::::

::: {.mobile-only-button-container}
```{=html}
<a href="packages/index.qmd" class="button btn">Explore Ecosystem &rarr;</a>
```
:::
<!-- Core Packages Section Ends Here -->


<!-- A model built from parts Section -->
:::: {.section-header .section-start-space}

::: {#model}

### A model built from parts
<p>Assemble a latent process, an infection model, and observation layers into one Turing model. The structure is clear — every part is named and stays inline.</p>

:::

```{=html}
<a href="tutorials/assembled-model.qmd" class="button desktop-only-button">Read the tutorial &rarr;</a>
```

::::

::: {.code-example-column .section-end-space}
::: {.code-window .box}
::: {.code-window-header}
::: {.code-window-dots}

```{=html}
<span class="dot red"></span><span class="dot yellow"></span><span class="dot green"></span>
```

:::

::: {.code-window-title}

```{=html}
<span>renewal_model.jl</span>
```

:::

:::

```{.julia .code-overflow-scroll}
using ComposableTuringIDModels, Distributions, Turing

# Renewal infections with inline latent process
data = IDData(gen_distribution = Gamma(1.4, 1 / 0.38))

model = IDModel(
    Renewal(data;
        rt = AR(; ϵ_t = HierarchicalNormal(
            std_prior = HalfNormal(0.1))),
        initialisation_prior = Normal(log(1.0), 1.0)),

    # Multiple observation layers:
    #   delay → ascertainment → negative-binomial noise
    LatentDelay(
        Ascertainment(
            NegativeBinomialError(),
            ascertainment_prior = Beta(10, 2)),
        truncated(Normal(5.0, 2.0), 0.0, Inf)))

# Fit to case data with Turing's NUTS
chain = sample(as_turing_model(model, cases, length(cases)),
               NUTS(), MCMCThreads(), 1_000, 2)
```

:::
:::
<!-- A model built from parts Section Ends Here -->


<!-- News & Updates Section -->
:::: {.section-header .section-start-space}

::: {#news}

### News & Updates
<p>Read the latest from the EpiAware team.</p>

:::

```{=html}
<a href="news/index.qmd" class="button desktop-only-button">View more &rarr;</a>
```
::::

::: {#news-listing}
:::

::: {.mobile-only-button-container}
```{=html}
<a href="news/index.qmd" class="button">View more &rarr;</a>
```
:::
<!-- News & Updates Section Ends Here -->


<!-- Built on Julia Section -->
::: {.section-header .section-start-space}

::: {#built-on-julia}

### Built on Julia
<p>We build in Julia for its type system, multiple dispatch, and automatic differentiation, on top of the scientific infrastructure.</p>

:::

:::

::: {.feature-grid .foundations .section-end-space}
::: {.feature-card}
### [SciML](https://sciml.ai)
Differential equations, sensitivity analysis, and scientific machine learning.
:::
::: {.feature-card}
### [Turing.jl](https://turinglang.org)
Probabilistic programming and Bayesian inference.
:::
::: {.feature-card}
### [Distributions.jl](https://github.com/JuliaStats/Distributions.jl)
The shared vocabulary of probability distributions our packages extend.
:::
:::

::: {.text-center .section-end-space}
New to Julia? Start with the [Using Julia](using-julia.qmd) guide.
:::

<!-- Background Section -->
::: {.section-header .section-start-space}

::: {#background}

### Background
<p>From R to Julia — the story behind EpiAware.</p>

:::

:::

::: {.section-end-space}
In R we built the [epinowcast](community.qmd) ecosystem and packages such as [EpiNow2](https://github.com/epiforecasts/EpiNow2) and [scoringutils](https://github.com/epiforecasts/scoringutils), now used by academics and public health teams internationally.
EpiAware is the Julia equivalent, a domain-focused ecosystem in the mould of SciML or Turing.jl, with community infrastructure like [rOpenSci](https://ropensci.org) and the domain focus of [SpeedyWeather.jl](https://github.com/SpeedyWeather/SpeedyWeather.jl).

We are at an early stage and looking for collaborators — [here is how to get involved](get-involved.qmd).
:::

::::::
